topic: Supreme Court and High Courts: Independence and Jurisdiction
subtopic: Appointment, removal, original, appellate, advisory, writ, review and supervisory powers

content.issueStory: Judicial independence depends on appointment, tenure, finance, contempt and institutional norms, while jurisdiction determines which court may hear which constitutional dispute. The Supreme Court and High Courts are constitutional courts with distinct original, appellate, writ and supervisory roles. Judges are formally appointed by the President under constitutional provisions shaped by collegium jurisprudence; removal requires a demanding parliamentary process.
content.coreConcept: The Supreme Court and High Courts are constitutional courts with distinct original, appellate, writ and supervisory roles. Judges are formally appointed by the President under constitutional provisions shaped by collegium jurisprudence; removal requires a demanding parliamentary process.
dimensions: Supreme Court original jurisdiction includes specified federal disputes under Article 131.
Appellate jurisdiction spans constitutional, civil and criminal routes plus special leave under Article 136.
Advisory jurisdiction under Article 143 is distinct from binding adjudication between parties.
Supreme Court Article 32 and High Court Article 226 writ jurisdictions differ in scope.
High Courts supervise subordinate courts and tribunals under Articles 227 and 235-related structure.
Security of tenure, salaries, contempt power and difficult removal support independence.
content.dataReport: Uploaded source basis: STATIC_09_Polity.pdf and PYQ_06_Polity_Governance.pdf. Refresh amendments, statutory rules, office status and later judgments from official sources: Constitution of India, Supreme Court and High Court portals and judgment databases.
content.caseStudy: High Court Article 226 jurisdiction is broader in legal cause than Article 32, while Supreme Court precedent declared under Article 141 binds courts.
content.pyqLink: UPSC patterns test Article-institution matches, exceptions, procedure and comparative statements within Supreme Court and High Courts: Independence and Jurisdiction. Resolve each statement through source, scope, procedure and consequence.

answerFramework.intro: Introduce Supreme Court and High Courts: Independence and Jurisdiction through the controlling constitutional principle and legal source.
body: Map the authority: Articles 124-147 and 214-231; Articles 32, 131, 136, 141-143, 226-227; Judges Cases.

Explain the first-order design: Supreme Court original jurisdiction includes specified federal disputes under Article 131. Appellate jurisdiction spans constitutional, civil and criminal routes plus special leave under Article 136. Advisory jurisdiction under Article 143 is distinct from binding adjudication between parties.

Analyse safeguards and tensions: Supreme Court Article 32 and High Court Article 226 writ jurisdictions differ in scope. High Courts supervise subordinate courts and tribunals under Articles 227 and 235-related structure. Security of tenure, salaries, contempt power and difficult removal support independence.

Use High Court Article 226 jurisdiction is broader in legal cause than Article 32, while Supreme Court precedent declared under Article 141 binds courts. and close with constitutional accountability plus dated legal verification.
answerFramework.conclusion: A strong Polity answer joins constitutional text, institutional purpose, judicial doctrine and a workable accountability safeguard.

prelimsFacts: Supreme Court is a court of record.
High Courts are courts of record.
Article 141 concerns law declared by Supreme Court.
Article 226 is broader than FR enforcement alone.
Judge removal requires parliamentary special-majority process.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the controlling rule in Supreme Court and High Courts: Independence and Jurisdiction?
revisionBox.answer: The Supreme Court and High Courts are constitutional courts with distinct original, appellate, writ and supervisory roles. Judges are formally appointed by the President under constitutional provisions shaped by collegium jurisprudence; removal requires a demanding parliamentary process.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the primary constitutional or legal authority?
What is the fastest elimination trap?
revisionBox.answer: Do not call advisory opinion identical to ordinary binding decree.

revisionBox.prompt: Where should later legal changes be verified?
revisionBox.answer: Constitution of India, Supreme Court and High Court portals and judgment databases

mindmap_json.center: Supreme Court and High Courts: Independence and Jurisdiction

branches.label: Text
children: Supreme Court original jurisdiction includes specified federal disputes under Article 131.
Appellate jurisdiction spans constitutional, civil and criminal routes plus special leave under Article 136.

branches.label: Institution
children: Advisory jurisdiction under Article 143 is distinct from binding adjudication between parties.
Supreme Court Article 32 and High Court Article 226 writ jurisdictions differ in scope.

branches.label: Safeguard
children: High Courts supervise subordinate courts and tribunals under Articles 227 and 235-related structure.
Security of tenure, salaries, contempt power and difficult removal support independence.

branches.label: Traps
children: Do not call advisory opinion identical to ordinary binding decree.
Do not give Article 131 to High Courts.
Do not equate appointment with simple executive discretion.

High Courts are courts of record.
Article 141 concerns law declared by Supreme Court.
Article 226 is broader than FR enforcement alone.
Judge removal requires parliamentary special-majority process.
